summary: Ukrainian air defenses shot down 115 Russian drones on the night of 22 May, while Russian forces continued guided‑bomb strikes on Donetsk, Dnipropetrovsk and Kharkiv regions. Ukraine confirmed a drone strike on a Russian unit headquarters in Starobilsk, rejecting civilian‑target claims. Diplomatic and supply developments include a U.S. $108 M HAWK system sale and NATO’s deep‑strike exercise in London.
key_points: Ukrainian air defence reported shooting down or suppressing 115 Shahed, Gerbera, Italmas and imitation drones across north, south and east Ukraine on the night of 22 May (Source: @kpszsu/62415).

Russian President Putin denied that a Ukrainian drone strike on a college in Starobilsk was caused by air defence or electronic warfare, while TASS claimed the drone operators knowingly targeted the building (Source: @tass_agency/377091, @tass_agency/377111).
Ukrainian General Staff clarified the Starobilsk drone attack hit the headquarters of a Russian unit operating the drones, not a civilian dormitory (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30372).

Multiple Shahed‑type UAVs were observed over Konotop, Sumy, Poltava and the Black Sea (Orlan‑10) indicating continued Russian reconnaissance and strike capability (Source: @eRadarrua/92986, @eRadarrua/92983, @kpszsu/62447).

Russian tactical aviation launched guided aviation bombs on Donetsk, Dnipropetrovsk and Kharkiv oblasts, including a strike toward eastern Dnipropetrovsk (Source: @kpszsu/62436, @kpszsu/62437, @kpszsu/62421).
Ukraine reached a technical agreement to evacuate roughly 6,000 civilians, including 200 children, from Oleshky and surrounding villages pending a ceasefire (Source: @UkraineNow/81381).
The United States approved a sale of HAWK anti‑aircraft missile system equipment to Ukraine valued at nearly $108 million (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30358).

NATO conducted the “Arcade Strike” deep‑strike exercise from a London underground platform, rehearsing electronic‑warfare and drone‑suppression scenarios against a simulated Russian invasion (Source: @DeepStateUA/23530).

sections.executive_summary: Ukrainian air defences downed 115 Russian drones on 22 May, blunting a large‑scale Shahed swarm across multiple fronts. Simultaneously, Russian tactical aviation continued guided‑bomb strikes on Donetsk, Dnipropetrovsk and Kharkiv, while a contested drone attack on Starobilsk sparked propaganda battles. Diplomatic moves include a U.S. HAWK system sale and NATO’s deep‑strike rehearsal in London, underscoring escalating external support for Ukraine.

points: Ukrainian air defence units, supported by radar and mobile fire groups, intercepted 115 hostile UAVs (Shahed, Gerbera, Italmas) across northern, southern and eastern sectors on the night of 22 May, limiting damage to five hit locations (Source: @kpszsu/62415).

The General Staff confirmed that a drone strike in Starobilsk targeted the headquarters of a Russian drone‑operating unit, not a civilian dormitory, countering Russian claims of a civilian massacre (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30372).

Guided aviation bombs were launched by Russian tactical aviation toward eastern Dnipropetrovsk, Donetsk and Kharkiv oblasts, indicating a shift to precision strike tactics (Source: @kpszsu/62436, @kpszsu/62437, @kpszsu/62421).

Ukrainian forces reported multiple UAV sightings: four Shahed drones over Konotop, several over Sumy heading toward Chernihiv, and an Orlan‑10 operating over the Black Sea opposite the Crimean Peninsula (Source: @eRadarrua/92986, @kpszsu/62447).

Technical arrangements were completed for the evacuation of approximately 6,000 civilians, including 200 children, from Oleshky and nearby villages, pending a ceasefire agreement with Russian authorities (Source: @UkraineNow/81381).
The United States approved a $108 million sale of HAWK anti‑aircraft missile system components to strengthen Ukraine’s medium‑range air defence (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30358).
Ukrainian engineers continued constructing field fortifications in Donetsk and Dnipropetrovsk, enhancing defensive depth after recent Russian assaults (Source: @DeepStateUA/23529).
Ukrainian intelligence warned that Russian propaganda is inflating migrant numbers to sow chaos, a narrative echoed by senior Ukrainian officials (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30367).

President Vladimir Putin publicly rejected claims that a Ukrainian drone strike on a college in Starobilsk was caused by Ukrainian air defence or electronic warfare, insisting the incident was a Ukrainian act (Source: @tass_agency/377091).

TASS military analyst Andrei Marochko asserted that UAV operators deliberately targeted the Starobilsk college, describing the attack as a terrorist act and noting that long‑range drones are produced in joint Western‑Russian ventures (Source: @tass_agency/377111).

Russian tactical aviation deployed guided aviation bombs against Ukrainian positions in Donetsk, Dnipropetrovsk and Kharkiv, reflecting continued offensive air operations despite Ukrainian air‑defence successes (Source: @kpszsu/62436, @kpszsu/62437, @kpszsu/62421).

Russian forces reported the launch of Shahed‑type UAVs from multiple directions—including Kursk, Shatalovo, Oryol, Bryansk, Millerovo, Primorsko‑Akhtersk, and Crimea—demonstrating a broad launch corridor network (Source: @kpszsu/62415).
Russian propaganda continues to claim civilian casualties from Ukrainian drone strikes, aiming to undermine Ukrainian legitimacy and rally domestic support (Source: @UkraineNow/81391).
Moscow dismissed NATO’s 2030 deep‑strike exercise in London as a hypothetical scenario, emphasizing that no real threat to the Baltic states exists (Source: @DeepStateUA/23530).

Russian officials highlighted the limited stock of British drones, contrasting it with Ukrainian consumption of thousands per day, to argue for increased European drone production (Source: @DeepStateUA/23530).
Russian military statements indicated that the Starobilsk attack resulted in civilian deaths, though exact figures were not disclosed (Source: @tass_agency/377095).
Ukrainian air‑defence units downed multiple Shahed drones over the Donetsk region, preventing further strikes on key logistics hubs (Source: @kpszsu/62415).
Russian guided‑bomb sorties targeted the eastern part of Dnipropetrovsk oblast, striking near the city of Vilnohirsʹk and causing infrastructure damage (Source: @kpszsu/62436).

UAV activity was reported near Konotop and Sumy, with four Shahed drones observed moving westward toward Chernihiv, indicating a possible reconnaissance sweep ahead of ground operations (Source: @eRadarrua/92986, @kpszsu/62447).
Russian forces launched a wave of UAVs from Kursk and Oryol directions, aiming at eastern Ukrainian positions in the Kharkiv sector (Source: @kpszsu/62415).
Ukrainian engineers fortified the front line near Avdiivka with new field fortifications, enhancing defensive depth after recent Russian assaults (Source: @DeepStateUA/23529).
A Ukrainian HAWK system component delivery is expected within weeks, potentially improving air‑defence coverage over the eastern front (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30358).
Russian propaganda claimed civilian casualties from a Ukrainian drone strike in Starobilsk, a town near the eastern front, to influence public opinion (Source: @UkraineNow/81391).
Ukrainian intelligence reported increased Russian UAV reconnaissance over the Black Sea near the Crimean coast, suggesting preparation for maritime targeting (Source: @eRadarrua/92986).
Ukrainian forces intercepted Shahed drones launched from Bryansk and Kursk, protecting critical infrastructure in Chernihiv oblast (Source: @kpszsu/62415).
UAV sightings over Sumy and Poltava indicated Russian reconnaissance flights heading toward Chernihiv, with four drones observed near Lipova Dolyna (Source: @eRadarrua/92968).
Guided aviation bomb launches were reported toward the northern Donetsk sector, threatening supply routes near the front line (Source: @kpszsu/62421).
Ukrainian air‑defence units reported a successful engagement of a Shahed drone over the town of Priluki in Chernihiv region (Source: @mon1tor_ua/66897).
Russian propaganda continues to allege Ukrainian attacks on civilian targets in the north, aiming to erode morale (Source: @UkraineNow/81391).
Ukrainian engineers completed new fortifications in the northern Donetsk and Dnipropetrovsk areas, improving defensive positions (Source: @DeepStateUA/23529).
Ukrainian General Staff denied any civilian targeting in the LPR, emphasizing military objectives only (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30372).
Ukrainian forces prepared evacuation routes for civilians in the Oleshky area, which lies near the northern front of the Kherson peninsula (Source: @UkraineNow/81381).
Ukrainian air‑defence reported downing several Shahed drones over the southern region, including near the Black Sea coast opposite the Crimean Peninsula (Source: @kpszsu/62415).
Russian guided‑bomb strikes were launched toward the southern Donetsk oblast, targeting infrastructure near the city of Mariupol (Source: @kpszsu/62421).
UAV activity was observed over the Black Sea near the Tatarsk region, with an Orlan‑10 drone operating in the area (Source: @eRadarrua/92986).
Ukrainian forces continued fortification work in the southern front, focusing on the Zaporizhzhia region after recent Russian assaults (Source: @DeepStateUA/23529).
The Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant faced a near‑blackout, prompting IAEA negotiations for a local ceasefire to enable repairs (Source: @UkraineNow/81382).
Ukrainian evacuation plans for Oleshky include 6,000 civilians, many of whom reside in the southern Kherson region (Source: @UkraineNow/81381).
Russian statements claimed civilian casualties from a Ukrainian drone strike on a college in Starobilsk, a claim disputed by Ukrainian officials (Source: @tass_agency/377095).
Ukrainian air‑defence units reported a successful engagement of a Shahed drone over the southern front near the village of Romny (Source: @kpszsu/62445).

Ukrainian air‑defence forces shot down 115 hostile UAVs (Shahed, Gerbera, Italmas) across multiple fronts on the night of 22 May, demonstrating high‑intensity air‑defence activity (Source: @kpszsu/62415).

Russian tactical aviation employed guided aviation bombs against Ukrainian positions in Donetsk, Dnipropetrovsk and Kharkiv, indicating a continued emphasis on precision strike capabilities (Source: @kpszsu/62436, @kpszsu/62437, @kpszsu/62421).
Ukrainian forces reported a successful interception of a Shahed drone over Priluki, Chernihiv region, highlighting effective radar‑guided SAM coverage (Source: @mon1tor_ua/66897).
The United States approved a $108 million sale of HAWK anti‑aircraft missile system equipment to Ukraine, expected to enhance medium‑range air‑defence coverage (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30358).
Russian UAV operators allegedly targeted a college in Starobilsk deliberately, according to TASS, suggesting intent to strike civilian infrastructure (Source: @tass_agency/377111).
Ukrainian General Staff clarified that the Starobilsk drone strike hit a Russian unit headquarters, not a civilian dormitory, countering Russian propaganda (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30372).

NATO’s “Arcade Strike” exercise simulated deep‑strike and electronic‑warfare scenarios against a hypothetical Russian invasion, reflecting heightened air‑war preparedness (Source: @DeepStateUA/23530).
Russian UAV launch corridors spanned from Kursk, Oryol, Bryansk, Millerovo and Crimea, indicating a broad and diversified launch infrastructure (Source: @kpszsu/62415).

key_developments: Ukrainian air defence downed 115 Russian Shahed, Gerbera and Italmas drones across north, south and east on 22 May, limiting damage to five locations (Source: @kpszsu/62415).

Russian President Putin denied Ukrainian drone involvement in the Starobilsk college strike, while TASS claimed the operators knowingly targeted the building (Source: @tass_agency/377091, @tass_agency/377111).
Ukrainian General Staff confirmed the Starobilsk drone hit a Russian unit headquarters, not a civilian dormitory (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30372).

Guided aviation bombs were launched by Russian tactical aviation on Donetsk, Dnipropetrovsk and Kharkiv oblasts, indicating intensified precision strike campaigns (Source: @kpszsu/62436, @kpszsu/62437, @kpszsu/62421).
U.S. approved a $108 million sale of HAWK anti‑aircraft missile system components to Ukraine, enhancing medium‑range air defence (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30358).
Ukraine finalized technical arrangements to evacuate roughly 6,000 civilians, including 200 children, from Oleshky and surrounding villages pending a ceasefire (Source: @UkraineNow/81381).

NATO conducted the “Arcade Strike” deep‑strike exercise from a London underground platform, rehearsing electronic‑warfare and drone‑suppression against a simulated Russian invasion (Source: @DeepStateUA/23530).
sections.threat_assessment: Russian UAV and guided‑bomb capabilities remain robust, with diversified launch corridors and continued use of Shahed swarms, posing a persistent threat to Ukrainian airspace and infrastructure. Ukrainian air‑defence has demonstrated high interception rates, yet the volume of attacks suggests Russia will sustain pressure while seeking to degrade critical assets, especially on the eastern and southern fronts. External support, notably the forthcoming HAWK system and NATO training, will marginally improve Ukraine's defensive posture but may not fully offset Russian offensive momentum.
sections.regional_response: Western allies are accelerating arms deliveries, exemplified by the U.S. HAWK sale, while NATO’s London exercise signals a commitment to joint operational readiness. Ukraine is leveraging diplomatic channels to secure civilian evacuations and maintain international support, as seen in the Oleshky evacuation talks. Russia continues to employ information operations, denying civilian casualties and framing Ukrainian strikes as terrorist acts to sustain domestic legitimacy.
sections.intelligence_notes: Monitoring of UAV launch sites in Kursk, Bryansk and Crimea is critical for pre‑emptive air‑defence positioning. The confirmed targeting of Russian command headquarters in Starobilsk may indicate a shift toward high‑value strikes, requiring updated threat matrices. The influx of guided aviation bombs suggests Russia is prioritizing precision over massed artillery, necessitating reinforced hardening of logistics hubs in Donetsk and Dnipropetrovsk. Continued NATO exercises will likely improve Ukrainian interoperability with allied forces, enhancing future joint operations.
